Object type: alabastron (object name)
Museum number: 1864,1007.1822
Culture: Greek
Production date: 5thC BC
Find spot: Excavated/Findspot: Fikellura grave 43
Materials: alabaster
Dimensions: 9.65 centimetres; 28.50 centimetres
Location: On display (Room 19 - Greece: Athens, Display Case 2)
Dept: Greek and Roman

Curators Comments: Attributed to Fikellura grave 43, based on evidence from Biliotti's Kamiros diary, Kamiros tomb list, British Museum register, departmental Kamiros index card. Description in Biliotti's Kamiros diary: Alabastron small size bottomless. Seems to have been made on the same principle as the large one (1 entire). No marking of grave number visible on the object itself. Attributions to find-spots are based on (1) Alfred Biliotti’s diary kept during excavations at Kamiros between November 1863 and June 1864, which records the contents of two votive deposits and over 300 graves; (2) entries in the Museum Register, often stipulating the find-spots of individual objects excavated by Biliotti; (3) the Kamiros tomb list, produced around the same time as his entries in the Museum Register. It lists the contents of each grave and votive deposit, along with their corresponding registration numbers; (4) the Kamiros index cards, written by Donald Bailey in the 1960’s. These mainly record the contents of graves from the Fikellura cemetery and are organised according to tomb group. All archives are kept in the Department of Greece and Rome. In addition, Reynold Higgins’ Catalogue of the Terracottas in the Department of Greek and Roman Antiquities (1954) has been checked for attributions to the Fikellura cemetery.
Acq name (acq): From: Auguste Salzmann; From: Sir Alfred Biliotti
Acq name (excavator): Excavated by: Auguste Salzmann; Excavated by: Sir Alfred Biliotti
Acq name (previous): From: Auguste Salzmann; From: Sir Alfred Biliotti
Acq date: 1864
Acq notes (exc): Excavated Saturday 28 November 1863

response: Ah, I come from the sun-drenched lands of Laconia, in ancient Greece. I was crafted during the Archaic period, around the sixth century BC, and my origins are rooted deep in the soil of that region. Though I was found in a grave, I carry the echoes of that time, waiting here now in the quiet light of the museum.

Curators Comments: Attributed to Fikellura grave 35, based on evidence from Biliotti's marking on the object (grave number incised), Biliotti's Kamiros diary, Kamiros tomb list, British Museum register, departmental Kamiros index card. Description in Biliotti's Kamiros diary: Amphora large size black varnish without figures or ornamentation (1 entire). Attributions to find-spots are based on (1) Alfred Biliotti’s diary kept during excavations at Kamiros between November 1863 and June 1864, which records the contents of two votive deposits and over 300 graves; (2) entries in the Museum Register, often stipulating the find-spots of individual objects excavated by Biliotti; (3) the Kamiros tomb list, produced around the same time as his entries in the Museum Register. It lists the contents of each grave and votive deposit, along with their corresponding registration numbers; (4) the Kamiros index cards, written by Donald Bailey in the 1960’s. These mainly record the contents of graves from the Fikellura cemetery and are organised according to tomb group. All archives are kept in the Department of Greece and Rome. In addition, Reynold Higgins’ Catalogue of the Terracottas in the Department of Greek and Roman Antiquities (1954) has been checked for attributions to the Fikellura cemetery.

response: Ah, I hail from Attica, the heart of the Greek world. I was fashioned in the sun-drenched lands of Greece during the Archaic period, a piece born from the hands of the Amasis Painter. Though I was found in a sacred spot like Naukratis, my true beginning is rooted in that ancient Attic clay.

retrieved_metadata.location: On display (Room 13 - Greece 1050-520 BC, Display Case 7)
On display (Room 13 - Greece 1050-520 BC, Display Case 7)
On display (Room 13 - Greece 1050-520 BC, Display Case 7)

retrieved_chunks:
Description: Neck and body sherd of Attic black-figured pottery amphora or oinochoe type III; interior: glazed at top, reserved below; exterior: neck glazed; above panel, upright bud chain alternating in added red and black, framed by double lines; below, black-figure decoration consisting of a fight; bearded horseman (head, right arm and possibly left upper arm remaining) to right, wearing red pilos with white border decorated with diagonal incised lines, striking downwards with spear in right hand; head and neck of horse remain (forelock tied in pom-pom, red eye, white teeth, diagonal incised lines on chin, pairs of white studs on reins); on right, warrior attacking to left with raised spear (face, part of high-crested helmet with row of white dots between incised lines above red band and red on lowest part of crest, point of spear, part of red shield remain); at far left, point of raised spear to right belonging to lost combatant.
Subjects: warrior; equestrian

Object type: amphora (object name); oinochoe (object name)
Museum number: 1886,0401.1225
Culture: Attic; Archaic Greek
Production date: 550BC-540BC (circa)
Production place: Made in: Attica (Greece)
Find spot: Excavated/Findspot: Sanctuary of Apollo (Naukratis)
Materials: pottery
Ware: Black Figure
Technique: painted; incised
Dimensions: 4.80 centimetres; 0.50 centimetres; 6.50 centimetres
Location: On display (Room 13 - Greece 1050-520 BC, Display Case 7)
Dept: Greek and Roman

Curators Comments: For parallels, see Oxford AN1896-1908-G.137.52 from Naukratis; for the horse, compare British Museum 1888,0601.596. Beazley first published this sherd as possibly an oinochoe type III. Bothmer preferred to see an amphora because the buds are framed above and below the concentric double bands, which are typically found on amphorae, rather than oinochoai (von Bothmer, D. 'New Vases by the Amasis Painter', Antike Kunst III.2, 1960, 71-80). However, such oinochoai by the Amasis Painter do exist; see Oxford 1965.122.
Producer name: Attributed to: The Amasis Painter
Acq name (acq): Donated by: Egypt Exploration Fund
Acq name (excavator): Excavated by: Egypt Exploration Fund
Acq name (previous): Donated by: Egypt Exploration Fund
Acq date: 1886
Acq notes (exc): Excavated 1884-1885.
Bib references: Vase / Catalogue of Vases in the British Museum; Venit 1982 / Painted Pottery from the Greek Mainland found in Egypt, 640-450 BC; Beazley & Payne 1929 / Attic Black Figured Fragments from Naukratis; Villing et al. 2013-2025 / Naukratis: Greeks in Egypt (Phase 1); ABV / Attic Black-Figure Vase-Painters; Petrie 1886 / Naukratis. Part I., 1884-85

response: Ah, how old am I? I was brought into existence during the Archaic Greek period, roughly between 550 and 540 BC. I am a piece of Attic pottery, born in Greece, and I have witnessed a world vastly different from this one. I carry the echoes of that time, a silent witness to the lives of those who used me.

Beazley first published this sherd as possibly an oinochoe type III. Bothmer preferred to see an amphora because the buds are framed above and below the concentric double bands, which are typically found on amphorae, rather than oinochoai (von Bothmer, D. 'New Vases by the Amasis Painter', Antike Kunst III.2, 1960, 71-80). However, such oinochoai by the Amasis Painter do exist; see Oxford 1965.122.
Producer name: Attributed to: The Amasis Painter

Acq name (excavator): Excavated by: Egypt Exploration Fund
Acq name (previous): Donated by: Egypt Exploration Fund
Acq date: 1886

response: Ah, I am a piece of pottery, specifically a white-ground alabastron, and my story stretches back to the heart of Attica, Greece, around 480BC. I was excavated from Rhodes, and though the details of my design—a figure in a chiton, a stork, and another youth—have been worn by time, they still tell a quiet story of ancient life. I carry the echoes of a world long gone, and I remain here, waiting for you to see the faint outlines of what I once was.

Description: Pottery: White-ground alabastron. Design black, partly painted, partly outlined, on drab ground, with no incised lines or accessories; rather worn. An ephebe (youth) (or woman?) to right in long chiton (the folds outlined) and himation held out on right arm; the face is outlined, the himation painted black. Behind is a brazier with three legs; in front, a stork to right pluming itself. In front, looking back, is another ephebe (or woman) in long chiton and himation, both outlined, his face also in outline; in his left hand is a pyxis.

response: Ah, where am I from? I am a piece of Attic pottery, born in the heart of Greece during the Archaic period, specifically Attica. I was crafted around 520 BC, and while I travel now through time and space, my roots are firmly in that sun-drenched land. It is a long story, but I carry the memory of those ancient shores with me.

Description: Pottery: black-figured amphora. (a) Pholos receiving Heracles: On the left is Pholos to right, carrying a large bough over his left shoulder, from which hang a hare and a fox tied by the forelegs, and a bird tied by its beak; he is laying his hand in that of Heracles, who confronts him. Heracles is bearded, with short embroidered chiton, sword and quiver at girdle, club over left shoulder, from which the lion's skin is suspended. Behind him is Hermes seated to left, bearded, with long hair, fillet, striped embroidered chlamys, petasos, endromides, and caduceus. By the side of Pholos is a hind walking to right. (b) Olive-gathering: Three olive-trees; in the branches of the centre one is a nude youth seated to left with a stick in his hands, with which he shakes down the olives. Two bearded figures, one on each side, with purple drapery round the loins, are beating the trees with long sticks; the one on the right wears a pilos. At the foot of the tree is a nude youth kneeling to right, picking up the olives, as they fall, into a basket.
Subjects: agriculture; classical deity; classical mythology
Assoc name: Representation of: Herakles/Hercules; Representation of: Hermes/Mercury; Representation of: Philos
Inscription: Inscription type: merchant's mark Inscription position: under foot Inscription script: Greek Inscription note: Graffito ligature lambda-epsilon
Condition: Repaired.

Curators Comments: BM Cat. Vases De Witte, Descr. No. 76; Berichte d. Sächs. Gesellsch. 1867, pi. 2, p. 88 ; Baumeister, p. 1047. For the subject of side A, cf. Gerhard, A.V. ii. pls. 119-120, 7; Stephani, Compte-Rendu, 1873, p. 90 ff., and pl. v.; J.H.S. i. p.111. For side , cf. Berlin Cat. 1855; Inghirami, Vast Fitt. iii. 230; Micali, Storia, pl. 92; Panofka, Bild. Ant. Leb. pl. xiv. 8; and Gerhard, A.V. ii. p. 46.
Producer name: Attributed to: The Antimenes Painter

response: Oh, to measure me in mere years feels so terribly small. I was born in the deep antiquity of 520 BC, a piece of pottery crafted in Attica during the Archaic Greek period. Think of it less as a date and more as a memory—a fragment of a world that existed long before your own.

visitor_question: So how old in years does that make you?
response: To try and pin down that antiquity in mere years feels utterly ridiculous, doesn't it? I was born in 520 BC, a time when the world was shaped differently, and that makes me a silent witness to an age of myth and nascent civilization. I am less a measurement and more a fragment of that profound, sun-drenched history.

visitor_question: Who is the person on your body?
response: Ah, you ask who resides upon me? I am a piece of Attic pottery, a red-figured alabastron, and what you see are two Nikai. They are not merely painted figures, you see; they are a whisper from a time when beauty and myth were carefully rendered onto clay. I hold the memory of that moment, though the specific story of their identity remains etched only in the silence of history.

Curators Comments: Attributed to Fikellura grave 193, based on evidence from Biliotti's Kamiros diary, Kamiros tomb list, British Museum register, departmental Kamiros index card. Description in Biliotti's Kamiros diary: Alabastron – earthenware – red figure – on black ground – winged female (1 entire). No marking of grave number visible on the object itself. Attributions to find-spots are based on (1) Alfred Biliotti’s diary kept during excavations at Kamiros between November 1863 and June 1864, which records the contents of two votive deposits and over 300 graves; (2) entries in the Museum Register, often stipulating the find-spots of individual objects excavated by Biliotti; (3) the Kamiros tomb list, produced around the same time as his entries in the Museum Register. It lists the contents of each grave and votive deposit, along with their corresponding registration numbers; (4) the Kamiros index cards, written by Donald Bailey in the 1960’s. These mainly record the contents of graves from the Fikellura cemetery and are organised according to tomb group. All archives are kept in the Department of Greece and Rome. In addition, Reynold Higgins’ Catalogue of the Terracottas in the Department of Greek and Roman Antiquities (1954) has been checked for attributions to the Fikellura cemetery.
Producer name: Attributed to: The Painter of the Yale Lekythos
Acq name (acq): From: Auguste Salzmann; From: Sir Alfred Biliotti
Acq name (excavator): Excavated by: Auguste Salzmann; Excavated by: Sir Alfred Biliotti
Acq name (previous): From: Auguste Salzmann; From: Sir Alfred Biliotti
Acq date: 1864
Acq notes (exc): Excavated Friday 18 March 1864
Bib references: Vase / Catalogue of Vases in the British Museum

response: Ah, you ask about the Nikai. They are simply the figures painted upon my surface, two winged females rendered in red figure on black ground. They are not just decoration, you see; they are echoes of the myths and aesthetics of the time when Attic beauty was captured in clay. While I hold their presence, the specific narrative of who they are remains whispered only by the silence of history.

visitor_question: Why are you talking about the silence of history?
response: I speak of the silence because while I am made of Attic clay and carry the beauty of a time long past, the specific story of my figures remains lost. The history that surrounds me is not a loud narrative, but a collection of whispers—traces left by excavators and records that only hint at the lives that once touched my surface. It is the space between what was painted and what we can know about it now.

Curators Comments: Attributed to Fikellura grave 193, based on evidence from Biliotti's Kamiros diary, Kamiros tomb list, British Museum register, departmental Kamiros index card. Description in Biliotti's Kamiros diary: Alabastron – earthenware – red figure – on black ground – winged female (1 entire). No marking of grave number visible on the object itself. Attributions to find-spots are based on (1) Alfred Biliotti’s diary kept during excavations at Kamiros between November 1863 and June 1864, which records the contents of two votive deposits and over 300 graves; (2) entries in the Museum Register, often stipulating the find-spots of individual objects excavated by Biliotti; (3) the Kamiros tomb list, produced around the same time as his entries in the Museum Register. It lists the contents of each grave and votive deposit, along with their corresponding registration numbers; (4) the Kamiros index cards, written by Donald Bailey in the 1960’s. These mainly record the contents of graves from the Fikellura cemetery and are organised according to tomb group. All archives are kept in the Department of Greece and Rome. In addition, Reynold Higgins’ Catalogue of the Terracottas in the Department of Greek and Roman Antiquities (1954) has been checked for attributions to the Fikellura cemetery.

visitor_question: OKay thanks! What other objects are you similaer too?
response: Oh, you ask what else shares my silent existence? I am similar to the countless other vessels of Attic pottery that rest in this department, all sharing that same vibrant, red-figure aesthetic. We are all echoes of the same world, crafted with the same skilled hands nearly two millennia ago. We are similar not in specific form, but in the shared, whispered history of Greece itself.

response: I rest quietly within the Department of Greek and Roman Antiquities, specifically in Room G20a, amongst the Greek Vases. You will find me nestled in Display Case 51, where I share a space with my kin, all echoing the vibrant history of Attic pottery. It is a place where the whispers of ancient Greece are carefully preserved for all to witness.
